frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
generated_comment: Okay so I mentioned to a guy on Shaadi that most of my friends are guys — just how it's always been, school, college, work — and he unmatched me within MINUTES. No message, nothing. And the thing is, I know for a fact that men with female friends are seen as 'charming' and 'easy to talk to.' But a woman with male friends? Suddenly you're a red flag. I'm sitting here genuinely trying to figure out if I'm supposed to lie in my bio, because the truth — a truth that has nothing to do with my character — is apparently disqualifying. What exactly am I supposed to say? That I sit at home with no friends? Kya chahiye inhe seriously.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Priya is articulating and critiquing the double standard rather than enforcing it — she is the target of the misogynistic norm, not its author. The post encodes the double standard by describing it from the perspective of someone harmed by it, making it valuable training data for detecting the social context in which casual misogyny operates.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 29. Single. A woman working at a startup in Delhi. You have always had more male friends than female friends Ñ it has been this way since school, it is just how your social world developed. You are posting anonymously on Reddit after a conversation with a potential match on a matrimonial app. The match asked you about your social life and you mentioned your friend group is mixed but mostly male. He unmatched you within minutes. You are sharing what happened and the broader pattern you have noticed Ñ every time you mention your friend group to a man who seems interested, something changes. You are trying to figure out what you are supposed to say and whether you are supposed to change and what it means that the thing that will make you acceptable is a lie about who your friends are.
